message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 18
Recent changes: Added enchantmentâ‰Ą8 â†’ 0.5 reimbursement after dragonâ†’1 in itemReimbursementFraction

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll perform the Refactor phase. Let me start by reading the architecture notes and the current state of the code.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base Ă— first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000â€¦01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers â€” speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table â€” zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again â€” still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` â†’ `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup â†’ item-modifier multiplier â†’ first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` â€” having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` â†’ `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup â†’ item-modifier multiplier â†’ first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
26	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver â€” the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 â†’ ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body â€” both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100Ă—1.5Ă—1.3Ă—1.1 = 214.5 â†’ roundâ†’215, ceilâ†’215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 Ă— 0.80 Ă— 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil â†’ 53, +5 = 58 â€” also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the roundâ†’ceil switch until a test actually fails.
27	- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere â€” claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
31	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8â€“9 (loyalty discount: `100 Ă— 0.80 Ă— 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 Ă— 0.80 Ă— 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 Ă— 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil â†’ 28) â€” every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
32	- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario â€¦ second amulet quote = 60 Ă— 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency â€” extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 Ă— 0.80 Ă— 1.10 = 52.8 â†’ ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work â€” round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`; replaced magic `>= 2` in the loyalty ternary with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline â€” every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. The spec's domain phrase "loyal customer â‰Ą2 years" now reads in the code. Mass impact negligible (+1 binding, -1 constant from inline literal moving to named binding). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (cycle-8 reasoning unchanged â€” the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature, so extracting now locks in the wrong shape). Cycle 10 (rune 33) is a pure `BASE_PRICE` table addition â€” zero edits to `runScenario` predicted.
40	- Naming established: numeric thresholds that gate a multiplier follow the `*_THRESHOLD` suffix (sibling to `*_MULTIPLIER`); both stand together to read as "if customer crosses THRESHOLD, apply MULTIPLIER".
41	
42	## After cycle 10
43	- Change: reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries from insertion order (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25) to descending-by-value (sword 100, staff 80, amulet 60, potion 40, rune 25). `runScenario` body untouched. Cycle-10 delta itself (`rune: 25`) was absorbed by the cycle-2 lookup table with zero edits to the pricing pipeline, as predicted in cycle 9.
44	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), presentation tier. With five entries now, insertion order no longer carries meaning (sword and amulet were just the first two test cases); descending-by-value lets a reader scan the price ladder at a glance and immediately see which items are cheap (rune) vs premium (sword). Zero behavioural risk â€” `Record<string, number>` key order doesn't affect lookup semantics. Mass unchanged (~42). Resisted (again) extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: cycles 11â€“13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price â€” `priceForItem` with an `Item` signature locks in the wrong shape because building blocks operate on item *groups*, not single items. Cycle 14 (two-quote scenario with post-first-contract discount 0.85) introduces a quote-index dependency that will force the customer-discount extraction with the right signature. Defer both extractions until those tests provide the driver.
45	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` table is sorted descending by value; new entries (moonstone in cycle 13) should be inserted at the correct position to preserve the price ladder.
46	
47	## After cycle 11
48	- Change: extracted module constant `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE = 60` with a comment explaining that three alike "building-block" pieces (rune, moonstone, â€¦) pool to a single unit priced at this fixed tier, independent of the per-piece `BASE_PRICE`. Replaced inline `buildingBlocks * 60` with `buildingBlocks * BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE`. Otherwise `runScenario` body structure (rune-count â†’ buildingBlocks/looseRunes â†’ loop excluding rune â†’ add pooled+loose contribution) untouched.
49	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The bare `60` was the only remaining unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline AND it was actively misleading â€” a reader could plausibly read it as `BASE_PRICE.amulet` (which is also 60), conflating two semantically distinct rules. The comment + named constant disambiguates: building-block tier â‰  amulet base. This matters because cycle 13 (three moonstones also pool to 60) will confirm the rule generalises beyond runes â€” moonstone base will be different, but its 3-pool price will also be 60, so the constant carries its semantics correctly across types. Mass impact ~0 (binding+invocation in vs. literal+(inline) out is a wash). Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)`: cycle 12 (four runes = 1 block + 1 loose) is absorbed by the existing block/loose split with zero changes; cycle 13 (rune AND moonstone, two pooled types) is the real driver â€” that's when the hardcoded `i.type === "rune"` check on lines 38 and 43 becomes a duplicated string-literal type test against a hypothetical `POOLED_TYPES` set, and the extraction signature can be designed against two concrete examples instead of one. Extracting now with only rune in mind would lock the API around a single-pooled-type assumption.
50	- Naming established: `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE` is the pool-tier price for any three-alike grouping (rune today, moonstone in cycle 13); it is intentionally separate from `BASE_PRICE` (which is per-piece) even when they share a numeric value. The "pooled types" set is currently inlined as the literal string `"rune"` in two places â€” cycle 13 will need to convert these to a shared `POOLED_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` or equivalent.
51	
52	## After cycle 12
53	- Change: extracted `quoteItemsSubtotal(items: Item[]): number` containing the entire per-quote item-pricing block (rune-count â†’ buildingBlocks/looseRunes â†’ loop excluding rune â†’ add pooled+loose contribution). `runScenario` body collapses to four readable lines: destructure â†’ `quoteItemsSubtotal(steps[0].items)` â†’ `loyaltyMultiplier` ternary â†’ premium formula (`ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemsTotal Ă— loyalty Ă— first-insurance) + ADMIN_FEE`).
54	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) finally has a driver â€” the cycle-12 four-runes test was absorbed with zero edits (as predicted in cycle 11), but the resulting body had reached 17 lines mixing two abstraction levels: (a) "compute per-quote item subtotal with pooling" and (b) "wrap subtotal in customer-loyalty + first-insurance + admin-fee envelope". The pipeline now reads as a four-step formula at the top level. Crucially, the helper signature `(items: Item[]) â†’ number` is the right shape for BOTH upcoming forces: cycle 13 (moonstone joins inside the helper, no `runScenario`-level change), and cycle 14 (sword+amulet in one quote â€” already absorbed: helper iterates all items), and cycle 15 (two-quote scenario â€” `runScenario` will map over `steps`, calling `quoteItemsSubtotal` per quote). Module mass net +3 (one new invocation, one parameter binding); `runScenario` body mass dropped from ~89 to ~30; helper carries ~60 with a single concern. Rule 2 > APP per project conventions, but here APP barely moves anyway. Still resisting `POOLED_TYPES` set / `priceForItemGroup` extraction â€” cycle 13's moonstone is the only correct driver for that, and lifting it now still locks the wrong shape (with only rune as exemplar the signature can't see the two-type generalisation).
55	- Naming established: `quoteItemsSubtotal(items)` is the boundary between item-level pricing (BASE_PRICE Ă— itemModifierMultiplier, plus pooling) and customer/contract-level pricing (loyalty Ă— first-insurance + admin fee). New per-quote pricing rules (moonstone pooling, sword+amulet multi-item, future bundle discounts) go INSIDE this helper. New per-customer/per-contract rules (post-first-contract discount in cycle 15) go in `runScenario` AROUND the helper call.
56	
57	## After cycle 13
58	- Change: merged the two passes over `items` in `quoteItemsSubtotal` into a single if/else partition â€” the pooled branch accumulates into `pooledCounts`, the non-pooled branch prices inline into `subtotal`. The follow-up loop over `pooledCounts` (block + loose finalisation) is unchanged. Module structure otherwise untouched (BASE_PRICE table gained `moonstone: 25`, POOLED_TYPES set added in Green; both correct as Green left them).
59	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) had a clear driver â€” the second loop existed only to repeat `POOLED_TYPES.has(item.type)` and dispatch the non-pooled half of the same partition Green had already started in the first loop. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): one loop, one conditional check, and one invocation removed (APP mass ~70 â†’ ~60 inside the helper). Rule 2 preserved: the if/else reads cleanly as "for each item, either pool it or price it inline", and the finalisation loop's role ("turn pooled counts into block + loose contributions") is now visually distinct rather than buried as a third loop after two near-duplicates. The structure also pre-positions cycle 14 (sword+amulet) and cycle 15 (two-quote scenario) just as well as the two-loop version did â€” both go through the unified partition without further edits. Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)` â€” the pooling finalisation is now only 3 lines and a single concern, and the right driver for that extraction is a future test where pooled-group pricing itself gets a modifier (e.g. cursed runes, or a different block size); none of cycles 14â€“24 introduces that.
60	- Naming established: the if/else inside `quoteItemsSubtotal` is the canonical "partition + price" shape for per-item pricing; new pooled types just join `POOLED_TYPES`, new per-piece pricing variations attach via `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` inside the non-pooled branch.
61	
62	## After cycle 14
63	- Change: renamed local binding `itemsTotal` â†’ `itemsSubtotal` in `runScenario` so it mirrors the helper name `quoteItemsSubtotal` it receives its value from. Cycle-14 delta itself (sword + amulet in one quote) was absorbed by the cycle-12/13 partition in `quoteItemsSubtotal` with zero edits to `runScenario` body, as predicted in cycle 12.
64	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), micro-tier. The binding represents the pre-loyalty, pre-first-insurance, pre-admin-fee figure â€” a subtotal, not a total. Calling it `Total` next to a `Subtotal`-named helper invited a reader to assume the helper was doing more than it does (or that the local was already the final premium). The two now share vocabulary: `itemsSubtotal = quoteItemsSubtotal(items)`. Mass impact zero (binding-name length is free in APP). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction: cycle 15 (the next test, two-quote scenario with second-quote `Ă— 0.85` post-first-contract discount) provides the proper driver â€” that test introduces both a second customer-side multiplier AND a quote-index dependency, so the extraction signature can be designed against the right forces. Cycle 15's two-quote shape will also require `runScenario` to map over `steps` instead of indexing `steps[0]`; that's a `runScenario`-body change, not a helper change.
65	- Naming established: local bindings that receive a helper's return value should mirror the helper's named role (`subtotal` here), not abbreviate or reinterpret it.
66	
67	## After cycle 15
68	- Change: replaced hand-rolled `let quoteIndex = 0; ... quoteIndex += 1;` mutable counter in `runScenario` with `Array.prototype.map`'s built-in index parameter â€” `steps.map((step, quoteIndex) => { ... })`. `POST_FIRST_CONTRACT_MULTIPLIER = 0.85` module constant and the `quoteIndex === 0 ? FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER : POST_FIRST_CONTRACT_MULTIPLIER` ternary that Green added stay as-is.
69	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements). The hand-rolled counter duplicates information `map` already provides â€” both count the same thing, but the mutable version requires a binding + initial constant + increment assignment that re-derives state the runtime hands us for free. Removing it drops APP mass in `runScenario` by ~15 (two assignments at mass 6 each + bindings/constants), eliminates the only `let`/mutation in the function (the body is now fully expression-oriented like the rest of the module), and makes the relationship "Nth quote in the steps array gets the Nth-quote treatment" structurally obvious â€” no need to trace the counter's lifecycle to confirm it tracks step position. Rule 2 preserved: `quoteIndex` keeps the same name in the new location so the ternary reads identically. The two-quote test still passes (115, 56) confirming map's 0-based index matches the hand-rolled counter exactly. Resisted (again) extracting `contractMultiplier(quoteIndex)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer, quoteIndex)` helpers â€” the upcoming claim tests (cycles 16â€“23) operate on a wholly different `op: "claim"` step type with deductibles, dragon-material, reimbursement percentages; the per-step dispatch is the real driver for restructuring how `runScenario` handles steps (it will likely become `step.op === "quote" ? quotePremium(...) : claimPayout(...)`), and at that point the quote-side helpers can be designed against the correct quote-vs-claim partition.
70	- Naming established: when a step-position index is needed, use the `map` callback's second parameter rather than introducing a parallel counter; reserve `let` for cases where mutation is essential to the algorithm (none exist in `runScenario` today).
71	
72	## After cycle 16
73	- No refactoring (justified): the first claim test (`{payout: 0}` for non-qualifying item) was absorbed by Green as a 3-line early-return branch (`if (step.op === "claim") return { payout: 0 };`) â€” minimal, well-localised, and structurally honest about what we know so far (nothing qualifies â†’ 0). Tried renaming `quoteIndex` â†’ `stepIndex` (more accurate now that the index counts both step types) and reverted: the cycle-15 naming convention is `quoteIndex`, the new name would surface a latent first-insurance bug (claim-before-quote would mis-classify the subsequent quote) that no spec test exercises, and the cycle-15 forecast explicitly anticipates the dispatch restructuring into `step.op === "quote" ? quotePremium(...) : claimPayout(...)` where the variable will correctly be a quote-position index again. Per cycle-12 pattern (resisted `quoteItemsSubtotal` extraction until the four-runes driver), extracting `claimPayout(step)` now with only a `{payout: 0}` stub locks the signature before reimbursement forces (deductible, dragon 100%, ench-9 50%, multi-item incidents, floor-at-zero, prior-quote item lookup via `policy` index) arrive in cycles 17â€“23 â€” the real shape needs at minimum `(claimStep, priorQuoteItems)` or similar, which one cycle of data can't reveal. Similarly resisted `quotePremium` extraction: pulling out only one side of an asymmetric dispatch is worse than the current early-return for Rule 2 (it implies symmetry that doesn't yet exist in the helper layer). Mass essentially unchanged (~50). The dispatch restructure becomes natural in cycle 17 when `claimPayout` does real work and the two branches have comparable weight.
74	
75	## After cycle 17
76	- Change: extracted `claimPayout(claimStep: ClaimStep, allSteps: Step[]): { payout: number }` containing the entire claim branch (policy lookup â†’ originating-item find â†’ reimbursement computation â†’ deductible subtraction â†’ floor at zero). Further extracted `itemReimbursementFraction(item: Item | undefined): number` returning the per-item reimbursement percentage (dragon â†’ 1, else â†’ 0). Added module constant `DEDUCTIBLE = 100`. `runScenario`'s `map` callback now reads as a clean two-case dispatch: `if (step.op === "claim") return claimPayout(step, steps); ... { premium }`, exactly matching the cycle-15 forecast shape.
77	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) had the real driver this cycle â€” the claim branch grew from 1 line (cycle 16's `{payout: 0}` stub) to 5 lines doing genuine work at a different abstraction level from the quote pipeline (claim reads the step history via `policy` index; quote reads only the current step). Continuing to inline both branches forced `runScenario` to mix "iterate steps to compute results" with "look up originating item on a referenced step" â€” two concerns the helper boundary cleanly separates. `claimPayout(claimStep, allSteps)` is the right signature for cycles 18â€“22: ench-9 50% (changes `itemReimbursementFraction` body, signature stable), multi-damage events (changes inner damage loop, signature stable), floor-at-zero edges (already handled), dragon-beats-ench precedence (changes `itemReimbursementFraction` body, signature stable). The sub-extraction `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` pre-positions cycle 18 (ench-9 50%) and cycle 23 (dragon beats ench-10) â€” both add cases to this single function, e.g. `item?.material === "dragon" ? 1 : (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= 9 ? 0.5 : 0`, with the dragon-first ordering encoding the precedence rule. Rule 3 (No Duplication): named `DEDUCTIBLE = 100` instead of inline `100` matches the established `*_MULTIPLIER` / `*_THRESHOLD` module-constant convention for every other numeric in the pricing/claims pipeline. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): the small bonus â€” `reimbursement = damage.amount * itemReimbursementFraction(item)` is a single multiplicative pipeline, structurally identical to the quote-side `itemPrice Ă— itemModifierMultiplier(item)` pattern; readers familiar with one immediately understand the other. APP mass: `runScenario` body dropped ~31 (claim branch collapsed from ~39 to 1 line), helpers carry ~50, net +~3 for function frames; Rule 2 > APP per project conventions but here APP barely moves either. Resisted extracting `quotePremium(step, quoteIndex, loyaltyMultiplier)` â€” the quote branch is already factored through `quoteItemsSubtotal` and reads as a 4-line formula at the right abstraction level; pulling it into a helper now would force a 3-parameter signature for symmetry-only gains (Rule 4 violation). Asymmetric extraction (claim-only) is honest about the asymmetry: quote pricing pipelines through item-subtotal then customer/contract envelope; claim payouts dereference the policy then compute payout â€” different shapes, different helpers.
78	- Naming established: `claimPayout(claimStep, allSteps)` is the boundary for all claim-side computation; new reimbursement rules (ench-9 50% in cycle 18, dragon-beats-ench precedence in cycle 23) attach via `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` INSIDE the helper; new damage-aggregation rules (multi-damage events in cycles 20â€“21) attach via the damage-loop INSIDE the helper. `DEDUCTIBLE` is the flat post-reimbursement subtraction, applied once per claim regardless of damage count (verified by cycles 20â€“21: 400-100=300, 300-100=200, each computed on the total claim's gross reimbursement). `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` returns the fraction in [0, 1] applied to the per-item damage amount; the function takes `Item | undefined` because `Array.find` may return undefined for non-qualifying items (cycle 16's case, where the find returns the item but it doesn't qualify; cycles 21â€“22's case, where damages include items not on the policy at all).
79

content.text:
All 18 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the refactor.

## Refactor Analysis

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

- `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` â€” current name accurately describes the return value (a fraction in [0,1]). It captures the new enchâ‰Ą8 â†’ 0.5 case as well as dragon â†’ 1.
- `claimPayout(claimStep, allSteps)` â€” still accurate
- All other names from prior cycles still fit

**Decision: Keep all names â€” they reveal intent correctly.**

### Step 2: APP Mass â€” Current State

Looking at the cycle-18 change site, `itemReimbursementFraction`:
```typescript
function itemReimbursementFraction(item: Item | undefined): number {
  if (item?.material === "dragon") return 1;
  if ((item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= 8) return 0.5;
  return 0;
}
```

Component breakdown:
- Constants: `"dragon"`, `1`, `0`, `8`, `0.5`, `0` = 6
- Bindings: `item` (parameter) = 1
- Invocations: `?.` access (Ă—2), `??` = ~3 (treating optional chain/nullish as ops)
- Conditionals: 2 if-statements + 1 nullish coalescing = ~3 â†’ mass 12
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Approximate mass: ~22 for this helper.

### Step 3: Look for Improvements

**Possible improvement 1: Extract `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD = 8` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_FRACTION = 0.5` constants.**

This matches the established convention: every numeric in the pricing/claims pipeline is a named module constant (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER`, `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER`, `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`, `ADMIN_FEE`, `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE`, `DEDUCTIBLE`). The new `8` and `0.5` are the only unnamed numerics in the module â€” exactly the pattern that triggered the cycle-9 `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` extraction.

The threshold `8` for enchantment-reimbursement is also distinct from the quote-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` threshold of `5` â€” naming both keeps that domain distinction visible.

**Possible improvement 2: rename `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER`** â€” cycle 6 noted that if a "low" tier ever appeared, we'd rename. The new enchâ‰Ą8 reimbursement is a CLAIMS-side rule (different concept from premium multiplier), so this is not a conflict. Skip.

**Possible improvement 3: Extract `claimItemFraction(item)` helper structure** â€” no, the helper is already clean.

The strongest improvement is **Rule 2 (Reveal Intent)** via extracting named constants. This is a direct echo of cycle 9's refactoring (loyalty threshold) and aligns with the project convention "every numeric in the pricing pipeline is a named module constant."

Let me apply it.

input.old_string: - Naming established: `claimPayout(claimStep, allSteps)` is the boundary for all claim-side computation; new reimbursement rules (ench-9 50% in cycle 18, dragon-beats-ench precedence in cycle 23) attach via `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` INSIDE the helper; new damage-aggregation rules (multi-damage events in cycles 20â€“21) attach via the damage-loop INSIDE the helper. `DEDUCTIBLE` is the flat post-reimbursement subtraction, applied once per claim regardless of damage count (verified by cycles 20â€“21: 400-100=300, 300-100=200, each computed on the total claim's gross reimbursement). `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` returns the fraction in [0, 1] applied to the per-item damage amount; the function takes `Item | undefined` because `Array.find` may return undefined for non-qualifying items (cycle 16's case, where the find returns the item but it doesn't qualify; cycles 21â€“22's case, where damages include items not on the policy at all).
input.new_string:
- Naming established: `claimPayout(claimStep, allSteps)` is the boundary for all claim-side computation; new reimbursement rules (ench-9 50% in cycle 18, dragon-beats-ench precedence in cycle 23) attach via `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` INSIDE the helper; new damage-aggregation rules (multi-damage events in cycles 20â€“21) attach via the damage-loop INSIDE the helper. `DEDUCTIBLE` is the flat post-reimbursement subtraction, applied once per claim regardless of damage count (verified by cycles 20â€“21: 400-100=300, 300-100=200, each computed on the total claim's gross reimbursement). `itemReimbursementFraction(item)` returns the fraction in [0, 1] applied to the per-item damage amount; the function takes `Item | undefined` because `Array.find` may return undefined for non-qualifying items (cycle 16's case, where the find returns the item but it doesn't qualify; cycles 21â€“22's case, where damages include items not on the policy at all).

## After cycle 18
- Change: extracted module constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD = 8` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_FRACTION = 0.5`; replaced the inline `>= 8` and `0.5` literals in `itemReimbursementFraction` with the named constants. `claimPayout`, `runScenario`, and other helpers unchanged.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `8` and `0.5` Green added were the only unnamed numerics in the module â€” every other numeric in the pricing/claims pipeline is a named module constant (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER`, `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER`, `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`, `POST_FIRST_CONTRACT_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`, `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE`, `DEDUCTIBLE`). This is the same Rule-2 pattern that triggered cycle 9's `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` extraction. The `_THRESHOLD`/`_FRACTION` suffix pair mirrors cycle-9's `_THRESHOLD`/`_MULTIPLIER` pair â€” a numeric gate and the value applied beyond it stand together at module top. Critically: the new threshold (8 for reimbursement) is intentionally separate from the existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER`'s gate (5 for premium loading) â€” premium-side and claims-side ench tiers are different domain rules with different thresholds; naming both keeps that distinction visible (a reader sees two different ench thresholds and immediately knows they're different rules, not one rule referenced twice). Mass impact ~0 (two literal constants â†’ two named bindings + two invocations; net wash). Resisted renaming `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` â†’ `MEDIUM_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` or similar to signal the existence of the higher 8+ tier â€” cycle 6 anticipated this but the rename would require updating spec test comments too and the current names are still accurate for their respective domains (premium vs claims). Cycle 23 (dragon beats ench-10) will exercise the dragon-first ordering already encoded in `itemReimbursementFraction`'s top-to-bottom `if` chain; zero edits to the helper predicted.
- Naming established: claim-side enchantment threshold lives under `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_*` namespace (distinct from quote-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER`). The `_THRESHOLD`/`_FRACTION` suffix pair signals "gate value + value-applied-beyond-gate" for reimbursement-style rules (analogous to `_THRESHOLD`/`_MULTIPLIER` for premium-style rules). Future tiers (e.g. if a low-ench partial-reimbursement appears) should follow the same naming pattern.
